finding: Maps GLP-1R-expressing cells across mouse brain; receptor present in reward-relevant regions but sparse/heterogeneous — establishes a substrate, not a behavioural direction.
Preproglucagon/GLP-1 neurons in the NTS project directly to VTA and NAc — anatomical basis for endogenous GLP-1 access to mesolimbic reward circuitry.
Central GLP-1R activation modulates cocaine-evoked phasic dopamine signalling in NAc — direct evidence GLP-1R engagement alters mesolimbic dopamine dynamics.
In humans, GLP-1R activation modulates activation of appetite- and reward-related brain areas to food cues (fMRI) — human target engagement of reward circuitry.
Longer-term liraglutide at obesity dose INCREASED reward-related OFC activation to food cues at weight-loss plateau — reward effect is not monotonic 'dampening' but time/context dependent.
Canonical GLP-1R signalling (Gs/cAMP/PKA) mechanism review — defines the molecular pathway but does not itself imply any reward/mood direction.
Comprehensive GLP-1 physiology/pharmacology review incl. central receptor distribution and signalling — background substrate evidence, direction-neutral.
GLP-1R signalling in the VTA reduces alcohol self-administration in male rats — regional causal evidence linking mesolimbic GLP-1R to reduced reward-seeking.
GLP-1R activation in NAc, ventral hippocampus and lateral septum reduces alcohol reinforcement in mice — multiple mesolimbic nodes reduce reinforcement.
Liraglutide attenuates the reinforcing properties of alcohol in rodents — clinically-used agonist reduces reward in vivo.
GLP-1R activation in the VTA attenuates cocaine seeking in rats — reduced drug-seeking, a craving-analogue endpoint.
Exendin-4 reduces cocaine self-administration in mice — cross-substance reduction in reward-driven behaviour.
Exendin-4 attenuates rewarding properties of psychostimulants (amphetamine/cocaine) in mice — conditioned place preference and locomotor reward reduced.
Exendin-4 decreases relapse-like drinking in socially-housed mice — models craving/relapse, reduced by GLP-1R agonism.
GLP-1R agonist treatment does NOT reduce abuse-related effects of opioid drugs — benefit signal is substance-specific, not universal; opioid reward may be GLP-1R-independent.

Part of food-intake suppression by peripheral GLP-1R agonists is mediated by nausea/malaise (conditioned taste avoidance) — the same pathway can produce aversive internal states, not purely 'reward dampening'.

Anorectic AND aversive effects of GLP-1R agonism are mediated by brainstem CCK neurons — aversion is a mechanistically separable, real consequence of GLP-1R activation (relevant to malaise/anhedonia concerns).

GIP receptor agonism attenuates GLP-1R-agonist-induced nausea/emesis — confirms GLP-1R activation drives an aversive axis that can be pharmacologically separated (mechanistic asymmetry within dual agonists like tirzepatide).

GLP-1 is BOTH anxiogenic AND antidepressant: acute central GLP-1 increases anxiety-like behaviour while chronic GLP-1 is antidepressant — explicit within-study bidirectionality; the only entry that carries a supports-harm signal (acute anxiogenic).
Liraglutide attenuates depressive- and anxiety-like behaviour in a corticosterone-induced depression model via hippocampal plasticity — animal evidence against a depressogenic mechanism.

RCT (MDD, n=72): semaglutide IMPROVED effort-based motivation (reduced perceived effort cost relative to reward) — directly argues against an anhedonia/amotivation harm mechanism in a depressed population.
RCT (MDD, n=72): semaglutide did NOT worsen depressive symptom severity and was reported safe; no signal of mood harm (executive-function primary endpoint null).

Meta-analysis of RCTs: GLP-1RAs modestly improved psychological well-being but had NO effect on depressive symptoms; authors judge any benefit likely INDIRECT (metabolic/general-health) rather than a direct mood mechanism.
Review: central GLP-1 implicated in both food and drug reward, positioning GLP-1R as a shared reward-modulatory node — integrative, hypothesis-level.
Review of brain GLP-1 in food-intake regulation and implications for GLP-1RA therapy — notes reward and aversion both engaged; direction depends on dose/circuit/timeframe.

ABSENCE OF EVIDENCE: No mechanistic study tests whether GLP-1R activation directly drives suicidal ideation or acute suicidality-relevant circuitry (e.g., serotonergic dorsal raphe, impulsivity/aggression circuits). The proposed harm pathway is UNSTUDIED, not refuted.

ABSENCE OF EVIDENCE: Whether GLP-1R-mediated aversion/anhedonia signalling (PMID:34844019, 22227019) can, in vulnerable individuals, translate into a mood-worsening or amotivational state at a clinically meaningful level is not directly tested — a plausible but UNSTUDIED harm bridge.

ABSENCE OF EVIDENCE: Rapid weight loss / caloric restriction as a mediator of mood change during GLP-1RA therapy is not mechanistically dissected in these studies — a candidate indirect harm/benefit pathway that remains uncharacterised.
